summary: This notebook demonstrates two distinct prediction pipelines for a biomass estimation competition: an embedding-based approach that extracts SIGLIP features and feeds them into tree ensembles, and an end-to-end approach that fine-tunes a DINOv3 ViT with a Mamba-style fusion neck. It emphasizes the practical value of semantic probing for frozen foundation models, the necessity of enforcing biological mass-balance constraints via orthogonal projection, and the use of gradient checkpointing with mixed precision to train large vision models on limited GPU memory. Readers should learn how to bridge tabular feature engineering with modern vision transformers, apply post-processing constraints to maintain domain consistency, and effectively combine heterogeneous model submissions to improve robustness.
competition_flow: Loads training/test CSVs and aerial images, extracts SIGLIP embeddings for tree models and fine-tunes a DINOv3 ViT with custom fusion heads, applies mass-balance constraints to predictions, and combines both submissions via weighted averaging to generate the final output.
data_reading: pd.read_csv for metadata splits and test CSVs
cv2.imread and PIL.Image.open for raw image loading
pandas pivot/melt operations to reshape long-format targets to wide and back
data_processing: Semantic probing via text prompts and cosine similarity scoring
PCA, PLS, and GMM feature projections with standard/max scaling
Orthogonal projection for mass-balance constraint enforcement
Image cleaning (cropping bottom 10%, inpainting orange date stamps)
Left/right image splitting and Albumentations augmentations (flips, rotation, color jitter)
TTA via horizontal/vertical flips and 90° rotation
features_engineering: Target max-scaling and derived mass-balance targets (GDM_g, Dry_Total_g)
models: vit_huge_plus_patch16_dinov3.lvd1689m (custom PyTorch model with LocalMambaBlock fusion and FiLM modulation)
loss_functions: biomass_loss (custom weighted sum of Huber and MSE per target)
ensembling: Weighted average of two submissions (SigLIP tree ensemble and DINOv3 ViT ensemble) with DINO-only predictions used for Dry_Clover_g, followed by orthogonal projection to enforce biological mass-balance constraints.
insights: Semantic probing can effectively extract domain-specific features from frozen foundation model embeddings without fine-tuning.
Enforcing mass-balance constraints via orthogonal projection is critical for biologically plausible and competition-ready predictions.
Gradient checkpointing and mixed precision are necessary to train large ViTs on limited GPU memory without OOM errors.
Combining embedding-based tree models with end-to-end vision transformers improves robustness across heterogeneous data distributions.
critical_findings: Dry_Clover_g is notoriously difficult to detect and is often fixed or handled separately to avoid prediction instability.
Orange date stamps on images cause visual artifacts that require inpainting to prevent model confusion.
Mass-balance constraints (Green+Clover=GDM, GDM+Dead=Total) must be applied post-prediction to maintain biological consistency.
Gradient checkpointing reduces VRAM usage by approximately 50% for ViT-Large, enabling feasible training on standard Kaggle GPUs.
